text: Wood smoke aerosol emitted during smoldering combustion was photochemically aged in a 104 m3 dual chamber system operated under varied relative humidity (RH) and NOx conditions. The mass light absorption of organic carbon (OC) aerosol was monitored using an integrating sphere-UV-visible spectrometer before and during photochemical aging in direct sun. It was observed that the color of wood smoke was decayed faster at higher RH, whereas its retention was slightly higher at higher NOx levels. This also suggests that NOx species affect chromophore formation through secondary processes. The mass absorption cross section of OC aerosol in the UV-Visible range (280-600 nm) was observed to be elevated by 11–54% during morning hours (after photochemical aging of 2–3 hr) and then started to fall-off to the baseline values by 19–68% during afternoon hours (after photochemical aging of 8–10 hr). Possible causes for this increase and fall-off in light absorption with time, due to the photchemical oxidation of wood smoke aerosol precursors may be attributed to increase in formation of chromophores and sunlight-induced bleaching. To confirm this a detailed study of composition of aged and fresh wood smoke aerosol precursors was carried out. PAH fluorescence intensities showed that fresh PAHs decreased with photochemical aging time, indicating that sunlight had bleached the fluorescent PAH species in the smoke aerosol precursors. Similarly, the concentration of levoglucosan a carbohydrate-like sugar used as an indicator of primary OA precursors, significantly decreased after photochemical aging suggesting chemical modification of smoke aerosol precursors. Aerosol water content studies using FTIR spectrometry suggested hygroscopicity decrease with photochemical aging of the smoke. Ambient OC aerosol was sampled 50 km off-shore of County Line wildfire in Northern Florida 2012 and post photochemical exposures exhibited similar pattern of changes as observed in chamber studies, thus confirmed that the biomass aerosol emissions has the potential of becoming less light absorbing after prolonged photochemical exposure i.e., after about 8-9 hr photochemical aging in direct sunlight.
